post: Move over, every famous duo in history. The greatest power couple in American political history was Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony. 70 years after the signing of the Declaration of Independence, Stanton shocked the nation at the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848, calling for women's suffrage and authoring the Declaration of Sentiments. The document was a deliberate reimagining of the Declaration of Independence: instead of targeting King George for trampling the rights of colonists, it targeted men for trampling the rights of women. It was a radical, fringe political statement. And it was going nowhere… Until Anthony. Where Stanton dropped philosophical bombs and went home to her 7 children, Anthony built the machinery. She was a relentless political organizer who took Stanton's ideas and turned them into a national movement, making women's suffrage seem "almost inevitable." Neither lived to see the 19th Amendment. But without their 5 decades of friendship and partnership, there is no women's suffrage movement in the United States. Episode 4 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/3NuU6bV  #WomensHistoryMonth  #SusanBAnthony  #ElizabethCadyStanton  #SenecaFalls  #WomensSuffrage  #America250  #CivicsEducation

One class raised funds to build a quiet corner in their school, a simple space for students who get overstimulated. Another student created an entire accessible tennis program for children with disabilities. He won the $10,000 grand prize. Both were recognized by the MyImpact Challenge. Both were valid. This is worth sitting with, because one of the most consistent barriers to entry, for students and for teachers, is the belief that a project has to be extraordinary to qualify. It doesn't. A lemonade stand fundraiser. A neighborhood cleanup. A book drive. An entrepreneurial club. A nonprofit. The MyImpact Challenge recognizes the full spectrum of student-led community impact, because the point isn't scale, it's that students take real, meaningful action. What's particularly worth knowing for educators: The MyImpact Challenge works exceptionally well as a whole-class project. Groups of up to 5 students can submit a single entry together. AP Government courses, service learning programs, and gifted/talented classes have all used it as an applied civics experience that gives students a real framework for the skills they're building in the classroom. There's $40,000 in total prizes, including awards for teachers whose students enter. Every student who submits a valid entry receives recognition. Students ages 13-19 in all 50 states, U. S. territories, and Armed Forces Schools Abroad can participate. If your students are doing the work, they deserve to be recognized for it. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #ServiceLearning  #APGovernment  #BillOfRightsInstitute

George Washington called for educating youth in the "science of Government" so they might become guardians of liberty. That was 1796. In 2026, on the eve of America's 250th birthday, 70% of American adults say they want more civic education. And yet civic knowledge keeps declining. Something's broken. And the time to fix it is right now. BRI President & CEO David Bobb and iCivics CEO Louise Dube co-authored a timely op-ed this Civic Learning Week laying out what's at stake, and what this anniversary moment demands of all of us. Read it here 👇 🔗  https://bit.ly/4uqHOlG  #CivicsEducation  #America250  #CivicLearningWeek  #DeclarationofIndependence  #BillofRightsInstitute  #iCivics

If you're a teacher, or if you know one, this is the moment to share it. During the American Revolution, women dared to write about politics in newspapers, sometimes under their own names. At the time, that was extraordinary. Mercy Otis Warren waged war against Britain with her pen, and at the urging of John Adams, wrote the history of the Revolution under her own name. Judith Sargent Murray followed in 1790 with On the Equality of the Sexes, arguing women deserved equal education and economic independence, not just for themselves, but for the sake of civic virtue. Their ideas became the backbone of Republican Motherhood, championed by Abigail Adams: women have a national duty to raise virtuous citizens, so women themselves must be educated. Therefore, women deserve equal access to education. None of them were arguing for suffrage yet. But they were building the foundation for those who would. Episode 3 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4svDEar  #WomensHistoryMonth  #FoundingMothers  #AmericanRevolution  #CivicsEducation  #America250

For more than 25 years, the Bill of Rights Institute has worked to equip teachers and students with the knowledge of our history, principles, and civic habits that our free society depends on. Today, we're taking the next step. Were introducing the BRI Civics Innovation Lab. The Lab is built on a simple conviction: the civics market has been underserved for too long. Not for lack of passionteachers have that in abundancebut for lack of tools that match the moment. This is the moment. The classrooms of today will shape how the next generation understands liberty, equality, and what it means to be a a good citizen. The Lab exists to make sure they get it right. Read the full story and see what the Civics Innovation Lab means for teachers, students, and the future of civic education:  https://bit.ly/4srtfwh  #CivicsEducation  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #AmericaAt250  #K12Education  #TeachCivics  #HistoryEducation

On March 4, 1865, Abraham Lincoln had every reason to gloat. The Civil War was nearly over. The Union had held. He had just won reelection, during wartime, against a general who wanted to negotiate peace with the Confederacy. He could have sounded triumphant. He didn't. Instead, Lincoln delivered one of the most morally serious speeches in American political history. He named slavery as the cause of the war, plainly, without equivocation. He acknowledged that all Americans, North and South, bore some share of moral responsibility. And then he asked a nation that had just lost 700,000 of its sons to choose reconciliation over revenge. "With malice toward none, with charity for all, let us strive on to finish the work we are in and to bind up the nation's wounds." That's not rhetoric. That's civic virtue: moderation and humility, exercised at the hardest possible moment. At the Bill of Rights Institute, we believe history education means teaching students to examine both when America lived up to its ideals and when it fell short. Lincoln's Second Inaugural does both. That's what makes it one of the most teachable primary sources in the American canon. Watch our Primary Source Essentials video on Lincoln's Second Inaugural and bring this moment to life for your students or your classroom 👇  https://bit.ly/3NaVq3D  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #PrimarySource  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #OTD  #AmericaAt250

The intellectual foundation of American women's rights wasn't written in America. In 1792, English philosopher Mary Wollstonecraft published A Vindication of the Rights of Women, and the argument she made was as simple as it was revolutionary: women are human beings. And human beings, by definition, are rights-bearers with equal moral standing. Enlightenment thinkers had already begun questioning whether women's subordination was natural or simply the result of tradition and denied opportunity. Wollstonecraft took that idea and drove it straight into the natural rights tradition: if all men possess rights simply by virtue of being human, then women, who are also human, possess those rights too. She rarely spoke of suffrage directly. She didn't need to. Her deeper claim, that women had a natural right to everything promised in the Declaration of Independence, laid the philosophical groundwork that shaped founding mothers like Abigail Adams, who called herself a follower of Wollstonecraft. Episode 2 of The Women's Declaration, our 10-part Women's History Month series with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG), is live now. 🎬  https://bit.ly/4sjt06j  #WomensHistoryMonth  #MaryWollstonecraft  #NaturalRights  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #America250
